lines.text: The gallery burned once. Not completely — the dungeon intervened at a specific point in the combustion. What survives is what the dungeon chose to preserve.

Ash and the faint smell of linseed. The oil paintings here were salvaged from fires both literal and institutional — works that burned, works that were burned, works that survived by being in the wrong place at the right time.
A masterwork requires a lifetime to create and a moment to destroy. The dungeon has preserved several that were given the moment before they could finish the lifetime.
Music notation on the walls — a composition that was never performed. The dungeon has performed it, in its own way, using frequencies the audience would not have enjoyed.
The archive chest in the corner holds manuscripts — drafts, final versions, alternative endings, discarded chapters. The published works were simpler. The chest contains what the work actually was.
The curator of this level made decisions about what to preserve. Those decisions are visible in what's here and what's absent. The absences are as interesting as the artifacts.
Canvas and char. The dungeon's art level smells like a museum that had a small, controlled disaster, and then continued operating anyway.

A forgery is in the collection. The dungeon knows which one. It has not labeled it. The quality of the forgery is indistinguishable from the originals. This is, the dungeon considers, part of what makes art interesting.
Several works in this level are unfinished — left mid-stroke, mid-sentence, mid-chord. The dungeon sealed them at the point of interruption. It finds incomplete works more honest than completed ones.
The primary enemy of masterworks is not fire or water or time — it is indifference. The dungeon is not indifferent. This is why the collection here is better than the one above.
